title: Random Geometric Graphs
speaker: Aleksa Milojević
abstract: The random geometric graph Geo_d(n,p) is a probability distribution over graphs, constructed by placing n points uniformly on a d-dimensional sphere and connecting two points whenever they are sufficiently close — where the proximity threshold is chosen so that each edge appears with probability p. Recently, Ma, Shen, and Xie used this model to obtain improved constructions for the Ramsey numbers R(k,Ck), where C>1 is a fixed constant. In this talk, I will present a simplification of their approach that allows for a sharper quantitative analysis and gives improved lower bounds.I will also discuss how the same ideas can be used to make progress on a conjecture of Bubeck, Ding, Eldan, and Rácz. They observed that when the dimension d is sufficiently large as a function of n and p, the distribution Geo_d(n,p) converges to the classical Erdős–Rényi random graph G(n,p) in total variation distance, and conjectured that the precise threshold for this convergence is d=(np)^3polylog(p). Until now, this conjecture had been verified only in the extremal regimes p=Θ(1) and p=Θ(1/n). I will outline a proof of the conjecture in the intermediate regime n^{-1/5}<p<1.This talk is based on the joint work with Zach Hunter and Benny Sudakov.

proof_stages.label: Random-geometric graph models
proof_stages.summary: The talk contrasts the binomial random graph with random geometric graphs, fixes the high-dimensional spherical model, and then switches to a Gaussian inner-product version whose red and blue edge probabilities match p and 1-p.

proof_stages.label: Erdos Ramsey lower-bound benchmark
proof_stages.summary: Ramsey numbers are defined and the classical probabilistic lower bound is derived by coloring a G(n,p) graph red, bounding expected red and blue cliques, and optimizing the critical edge probability.

proof_stages.label: Geometric off-diagonal Ramsey target
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the recent off-diagonal improvement obtained by replacing the Erdos-Renyi coloring with a spherical random geometric coloring, situating it relative to later improvements and the unchanged diagonal case.

proof_stages.label: Cap-conditioning mechanism for red cliques
proof_stages.summary: The key intuition is that once several vertices are constrained to be red-neighbors of an earlier vertex, they lie in a smaller geometric region and are therefore less likely to be mutually far apart, lowering red clique probabilities.

proof_stages.label: Gram-Schmidt Gaussian coordinates
proof_stages.summary: A Gram-Schmidt change of coordinates triangularizes the sampled Gaussian vectors so that the off-diagonal coordinates are independent one-dimensional Gaussians and inner products become explicitly computable.

proof_stages.label: Per-triangle clique corrections
proof_stages.summary: Using truncated-Gaussian estimates, the red triangle probability is computed to be smaller than p^3 by a first-order 1/sqrt(d) correction, which extends to one correction per triangle in red and blue cliques and yields a net off-diagonal Ramsey gain when p<1/2.

proof_stages.label: Signed-triangle testing threshold
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the total-variation testing problem between spherical random geometric graphs and G(n,p), explains the centered signed triangle count that distinguishes them below the conjectured dimension threshold, and reviews prior partial progress.

proof_stages.label: Typical-graph expansion for indistinguishability
proof_stages.summary: For not-too-sparse graphs and dimension above the signed-triangle threshold, the probability of each graph under the geometric model is expanded as its Erdos-Renyi probability times a factor governed by its signed triangle count, and typical concentration makes this factor 1+o(1), forcing total variation to vanish.

examples.name: Social acquaintance network
examples.note: The speaker uses acquaintances being more likely among nearby people as a motivating illustration for replacing independent-edge graphs by geometric models.

examples.name: Diagonal Ramsey case
examples.note: The case c=1 is used to illustrate why the geometric improvement is off-diagonal: at p=1/2 the red gain and blue loss balance rather than giving an improvement.
examples.engagement_reason: The diagonal Ramsey case is specialized in the bounds, with the speaker noting the square-root-two lower bound and checking that at c=1 the critical probability is 1/2 so the geometric gain does not improve it.
